title: Math — Circles
description: SAT-style circle questions with clean MC structure and deeply explained, step-by-step solutions.

questions.prompt: A circle has center \((-1, 1)\). A line is tangent to the circle at the point \((5, -4)\). Which of the following points lies on the tangent line?

solution.approach: Use the perpendicular relationship between a radius and a tangent line, then test which point satisfies the tangent line’s slope.
steps: The radius connects the center to the point of tangency, so it runs from \((-1, 1)\) to \((5, -4)\).
A tangent line is perpendicular to the radius at the tangency point, so its slope is the negative reciprocal: \(m_t = \frac{6}{5}\).
Test each choice by computing the slope from \((5, -4)\) to the candidate point and checking whether it equals \(\frac{6}{5}\).
solution.takeaway: At the point of tangency, the radius and tangent form a \(90^\circ\) angle, so their slopes are negative reciprocals.

questions.prompt: The length of arc \(\overset{\frown}{ADC}\) is \(5\pi\) and its central angle is \(100^\circ\). What is the length of the remaining arc of the circle?

solution.approach: Use the proportional relationship between arc length and central angle.
steps: Arc length is proportional to its central angle: \(\frac{s_1}{s_2}=\frac{\theta_1}{\theta_2}\).
The full circle is \(360^\circ\), so the remaining arc corresponds to \(360^\circ-100^\circ=260^\circ\).
solution.takeaway: When radius is unknown, compare arcs using angle ratios (proportions) instead of \(s=r\theta\).

questions.prompt: The equation \(x^2+x+y^2+y=\frac{199}{2}\) represents a circle. What is the radius of the circle?

solution.approach: Rewrite the equation in standard form by completing the square.
steps: Add \(\frac14\) twice, so add \(\frac12\) to the right side: \(\frac{199}{2}+\frac12=\frac{200}{2}=100\).
solution.takeaway: After completing the square, the constant on the right side equals \(r^2\).

questions.prompt: The equation \((x+4)^2+(y-19)^2=121\) represents a circle. Which value could be the \(x\)-coordinate of a point on the circle?

solution.approach: Determine the horizontal range of the circle using center \(\pm\) radius.
solution.takeaway: A coordinate on a circle must lie within one radius horizontally (and vertically) from the center.

questions.prompt: What is the center of the circle defined by \(x^2+20x+y^2+16y=-20\)?

solution.approach: Complete the square to identify the center directly.
steps: Standard form: \((x+10)^2+(y+8)^2=144\), so the center is \((-10,-8)\).
solution.takeaway: The signs inside \((x-h)^2+(y-k)^2\) reveal the center \((h,k)\) with opposite signs.

questions.prompt: A circle has radius \(18\). If the central angle of an arc is \(120^\circ\), what is the arc length?

solution.approach: Convert degrees to radians, then apply \(s=r\theta\).
solution.takeaway: The arc length formula \(s=r\theta\) only works when \(\theta\) is in radians.

questions.prompt: A circle has diameter endpoints \((2,4)\) and \((2,14)\). What is the radius of the circle?

solution.approach: Find the diameter length, then divide by \(2\).
steps: The diameter is vertical (same \(x\)-coordinate), so its length is \(|14-4|=10\).
solution.takeaway: When points align vertically/horizontally, distance simplifies to subtraction.

questions.prompt: Circle A has equation \((x+5)^2+(y-5)^2=4\). Circle B has the same center and twice the radius. What is the value of \(k\) in Circle B’s equation?

solution.approach: Track how changing radius affects \(r^2\) in the equation.

questions.prompt: What is the diameter of the circle \((x-5)^2+(y-3)^2=16\)?

solution.approach: Extract the radius from the equation, then double it.

questions.prompt: The measure of arc \(\overset{\frown}{RS}\) is \(100^\circ\). What is the measure of its central angle?

solution.approach: Apply the defining property of central angles.
steps: A central angle intercepts an arc, and its measure equals the measure of that intercepted arc.
solution.takeaway: Central angles match their intercepted arcs exactly.
